Serial Schedule Generation Scheme (SSGS):
Define the RCPSP input data: set of activities V (including dummy start/end), processing times p_i, renewable resource types R, resource capacities Cap_r, resource demands d_{i,r}, precedence relations (i,j) in A, and (optionally) release dates/earliest start ES_i and latest finish/other constraints if present.
Preprocess precedence constraints: compute for each activity i its set of predecessors Pred(i) and successors Succ(i).
Initialize SSGS state: set current time t = 0; set start time S_i = undefined and finish time F_i = undefined for all i; set remaining resource availability Res_r = Cap_r for all r.
Create the set of eligible activities Eligible = { i in V | Pred(i) is empty } (or all predecessors scheduled/finished).
Choose a priority rule (activity list) to order activities when multiple are eligible. Examples: highest priority index, earliest due date, largest resource demand, minimum slack, or any custom heuristic. Define how ties are broken.
Initialize an activity list/queue Q with Eligible ordered by the chosen priority rule.
Repeat until all non-dummy activities are scheduled (or until the end dummy is scheduled):
If Q is empty, advance time to the next event time when at least one currently unscheduled activity becomes eligible (i.e., when some scheduled activity finishes). Update the resource availability accordingly for the new time.
Select the next activity i from Q according to the priority rule (top of ordered list). Remove i from Q.
Compute the earliest feasible start time ES = max( F_k for all k in Pred(i) ) (respecting precedence). If release dates exist, set ES = max(ES, Release_i).
Find the earliest time t* >= ES such that scheduling activity i at t* does not violate renewable resource capacities for the entire processing interval [t*, t* + p_i).
To check resource feasibility for renewable resources, for each candidate time t* (starting from ES and increasing as needed), verify for every resource r that the cumulative demand does not exceed capacity: for all time points τ in [t*, t* + p_i), current_usage_r(τ) + d_{i,r} <= Cap_r.
Implement efficient resource feasibility checking using one of: (a) time-indexed arrays if horizon is small, (b) event-based profile (resource usage step functions) if horizon is large, or (c) segment tree / sweep-line to query maximum residual capacity over intervals.
Once the earliest feasible t* is found, set S_i = t* and F_i = t* + p_i.
Update the resource usage profile: for each resource r, add d_{i,r} to current_usage_r(τ) for τ in [S_i, F_i).
Mark activity i as scheduled.
Update eligibility: for each successor j in Succ(i), if all predecessors of j are now scheduled, add j to Eligible (if not already scheduled).
Rebuild or update Q by inserting newly eligible activities and maintaining the priority order according to the chosen rule. If the priority rule depends on dynamic information (e.g., current time, remaining work), recompute priorities as required.
Continue the loop until all activities are scheduled.
Compute the makespan (or objective value): for makespan, set C_max = max_i F_i (or use the finish time of the end dummy activity).
If the RCPSP variant includes additional constraints (e.g., non-renewable resources, time windows, minimum/maximum time lags), incorporate them into the earliest start computation and/or feasibility test for each candidate placement.
If the priority rule is not fixed, run SSGS multiple times with different priority rules or randomized tie-breaking to explore the solution space; keep the best objective value found.
Validate the schedule: verify all precedence constraints are satisfied (S_j >= F_i for each (i,j)), verify resource feasibility for all renewable resources over all intervals, and ensure no activity is scheduled more than once.
Return the final schedule (start/finish times for each activity) and the objective value (e.g., makespan).
Parallel Schedule Generation Scheme (PGS:
Define the RCPSP input data: activities V (including source/sink), precedence constraints (A→B), renewable resource types R with capacities Cap[r], nonrenewable resources (if any) with capacities and consumption, processing times p[i], and resource demands dem[i][r] for each activity i and resource r.
Choose the PGS variant and parameters: (a) activity selection rule (e.g., earliest start time, minimum slack, random tie-break), (b) priority function for tie-breaking, and (c) whether to use a fixed number of parallel schedule generation iterations or a single run with deterministic tie-breaking.
Initialize the schedule state: set time t = 0; mark all activities as unscheduled; set remaining resource capacities RemCap[r] = Cap[r] for all renewable resources; set current set of eligible activities Eligible = {i | all predecessors of i are scheduled}.
Initialize data structures: for each activity i store start time ES[i], finish time EF[i], and scheduled flag; maintain a list of currently running activities Running (for discrete-event simulation) and their completion times; maintain a mapping of resource usage for scheduled activities.
Create an empty schedule S (e.g., list of (i, ES[i], EF[i])).
Repeat until all activities are scheduled:
Compute the set of activities that can start at the current time t: for each i in Eligible, check feasibility with respect to precedence (already satisfied by definition of Eligible) and renewable resource availability: verify dem[i][r] ≤ RemCap[r] for all r. Collect feasible activities into FeasibleStart.
If FeasibleStart is empty: advance time to the next event time (the minimum completion time among Running), update t to that value, release resources of all activities completing at time t (increase RemCap[r] accordingly), remove completed activities from Running, and update Eligible by adding newly precedence-satisfied activities.
If FeasibleStart is non-empty: select a subset of activities to start in parallel at time t according to the PGS rule.
Apply the PGS selection rule to build the parallel start set: (a) order FeasibleStart by the chosen priority function (e.g., smallest earliest start, largest resource demand, random among ties), (b) iterate through this ordered list and tentatively schedule activities one by one at time t while maintaining resource feasibility: when considering activity i, if dem[i][r] ≤ RemCap[r] for all r, then include i in the parallel start set StartSet and decrement RemCap[r] by dem[i][r]; otherwise skip i (it remains eligible for later times).
After constructing StartSet, schedule all activities in StartSet simultaneously at time t: set ES[i] = t and EF[i] = t + p[i] for each i in StartSet; mark them scheduled; add them to Running with their completion times; record them in S.
Update Eligible after scheduling StartSet: for each newly scheduled activity i, reduce the unsatisfied predecessor count of its successors; any successor whose all predecessors are now scheduled becomes eligible and is added to Eligible (but it will only be started when resources allow).
Advance time to the next event: set t to the minimum completion time among Running (or to the next completion event if multiple finish at the same time). Release resources for all activities completing at the new time, update RemCap[r], remove them from Running, and continue the loop.
When all activities are scheduled, compute the makespan (or objective value): for RCPSP typically makespan = EF[sink] (or max EF[i] over all activities).
If using multiple PGS runs (recommended for stochastic tie-breaking): repeat the entire procedure with different random tie-breaks or different priority parameters, and keep the best schedule found (minimum makespan).
Validate feasibility: ensure all precedence constraints are satisfied and that renewable resource capacities were never exceeded at any time (by construction in PGS). If nonrenewable resources exist, additionally ensure total consumption does not exceed capacity (may require feasibility checks at selection time).
Return the best schedule S and its objective value (makespan), along with start/finish times for all activities.
List Scheduling with Priority Rules (LSP:
1) Model the RCPSP: define activities V={1..n}, renewable resource types R={1..m}, nonrenewable resources (if any), processing times p_i, resource demands d_{i,r}, precedence constraints (i,j) in E, and the project horizon/termination condition.
2) Choose the scheduling representation: use a time-indexed or event-based schedule where each activity has a start time S_i and finish time F_i=S_i+p_i.
3) Precompute precedence feasibility: for each activity i, store its immediate predecessors Pred(i) and successors Succ(i).
4) Compute initial earliest start times (optional but helpful): for each activity i, compute ES_i as the maximum over predecessors j of F_j (initially using topological order with dummy start).
5) Select a priority rule (or priority function) to rank eligible activities. Examples: (a) Largest processing time (LPT), (b) Most remaining work, (c) Minimum slack, (d) Earliest due date (if due dates exist), (e) Criticality-based priority using latest start times, (f) Highest resource demand, (g) Highest number of successors.
6) If using criticality/slack rules, compute latest start times (LS) and/or slack: (a) compute a backward pass using precedence graph and a chosen makespan estimate (e.g., from a forward pass), (b) derive LS_i and slack_i=LS_i-ES_i, and (c) define priority as a function of slack (e.g., smaller slack => higher priority).
7) Initialize the schedule state: set current time t=0; mark all activities as unscheduled; set remaining resource capacities Cap_r to their initial values (for renewable resources) and track nonrenewable consumption if applicable.
8) Build an initial eligible set Eligible = { i | Pred(i) are all scheduled } (activities whose predecessors are complete).
9) Define the feasibility test for starting an activity i at time t: (a) precedence feasibility is satisfied by construction of Eligible, (b) renewable resource feasibility requires that for all r in R, the resource usage profile over [t, t+p_i) does not exceed Cap_r, and (c) for nonrenewables, ensure total consumption up to the start (or throughout) does not exceed limits.
10) Maintain a resource usage profile: implement a data structure to query and update resource usage over time intervals (e.g., segment tree, difference arrays with event points, or a discrete-time array if horizon is small).
11) Main loop condition: repeat until all activities are scheduled.
12) If Eligible is empty: advance time t to the next event time when some currently running activity completes (or when precedence becomes satisfied), update resource availability accordingly, then recompute Eligible.
13) If Eligible is non-empty: sort or select activities from Eligible according to the chosen priority rule (highest priority first).
14) Attempt to schedule the highest-priority eligible activity i at the current time t: check resource feasibility for i at t using the resource usage profile.
15) If feasible: schedule i by setting S_i=t and F_i=t+p_i; update the resource usage profile for [t, F_i); mark i as scheduled; remove i from Eligible; add i’s successors to Eligible if all their predecessors are now scheduled.
16) If not feasible: either (a) try the next activity in the priority order at the same time t, or (b) if your LSP variant uses a strict single-choice policy, postpone i by moving to the next time when it becomes feasible. Prefer the common variant: iterate through Eligible in priority order until you find a feasible activity.
17) If no activity in Eligible is feasible at time t: advance time t to the next completion event (earliest finishing time among currently running activities), update resource availability, then recompute which activities remain eligible and repeat the feasibility checks.
18) Continue the loop until all activities are scheduled.
19) Ensure termination and correctness: verify that every scheduled activity respects precedence constraints (predecessors finish before start) and resource constraints (no renewable capacity violation; nonrenewable limits respected).
20) Compute objective values: typically makespan C_max = max_i F_i. If other objectives exist (e.g., total cost, resource leveling), compute them from the final schedule.
21) (Optional) Improve solution quality: run multiple iterations with different priority rules, tie-breaking strategies, or randomized tie-breaking among equal priorities; keep the best schedule.
22) (Optional) Add tie-breaking rules: when priorities are equal, break ties using secondary criteria such as earliest ES, largest remaining successors, or smallest processing time.
23) (Optional) Use lookahead for priority computation: for rules based on remaining work or criticality, recompute priorities dynamically as time advances (dynamic LSP) rather than only once at initialization.
24) Output the final schedule: report S_i and F_i for all activities, the resource usage profile (if needed), and the objective value (e.g., makespan).
Earliest Start Time Priority Rule (EST):
1. Input the RCPSP data: set of activities N (including dummy start/end if used), processing times p[i], renewable resource types R, resource capacities cap[r], resource demands dem[i][r], precedence relations (i -> j), and (optionally) release dates or time horizon T.
2. Build the precedence graph: for each activity j, compute its predecessor set Pred[j] from the given precedence relations.
3. Initialize scheduling state: set current time t = 0; mark all non-dummy activities as unscheduled; for each activity i set start[i] = null and finish[i] = null.
4. Compute initial eligible set E: include all activities i whose predecessors are all scheduled (for a typical RCPSP with a dummy start, this means Pred[i] is empty after considering the dummy).
5. Initialize a resource feasibility structure: choose a method to test resource availability over time (e.g., time-indexed capacity check, or an event-based/segment-based profile). Create a resource usage profile usage[r](τ) (or equivalent) initialized to 0 for all τ in the considered horizon.
6. Define the EST priority rule: at each decision point, among all eligible activities in E, select the activity k with the smallest earliest feasible start time (EST[k]). If ties occur, break them using a secondary rule (e.g., smallest activity index, largest processing time, or smallest slack).
7. For each eligible activity i in E, compute its earliest feasible start time EST[i] as follows: (a) precedence constraint: let est_prec = max_{j in Pred[i]} finish[j] (or 0 if no predecessors). (b) resource constraint: find the smallest time t >= est_prec such that scheduling i from t to t + p[i] does not violate any renewable resource capacity for all τ in [t, t + p[i]).
8. Resource feasibility test (renewable resources): for each candidate start time t, verify for every resource r that for all time points τ in the activity’s execution window, usage[r](τ) + dem[i][r] <= cap[r]. If using a time-indexed model, check all discrete τ; if using an event-based model, check all affected intervals where usage changes.
9. Select the activity to schedule: choose k in E with minimum EST[k] (computed in Step 7). If multiple activities share the same minimum EST, apply the chosen tie-break rule.
10. Schedule the selected activity k: set start[k] = EST[k] and finish[k] = start[k] + p[k].
11. Update resource usage profile: for each resource r, increase usage[r](τ) by dem[k][r] for all τ in [start[k], finish[k]). If using an event-based profile, insert/update the corresponding usage segments.
12. Update precedence eligibility: mark k as scheduled. For each successor j of k, if all predecessors of j are now scheduled, add j to the eligible set E.
13. Remove scheduled activity from eligibility: remove k from E.
14. Repeat Steps 7–13 until all activities are scheduled (i.e., E becomes empty and the scheduled set includes all non-dummy activities).
15. Compute the makespan: set makespan = finish[EndDummy] if using a dummy end activity; otherwise makespan = max_i finish[i] over all activities.
16. (Optional) Validate feasibility: confirm all precedence constraints are satisfied and all renewable resource capacities are never exceeded throughout the schedule.
17. (Optional) Improve solution quality: if desired, run multiple passes with different tie-break rules, or use the EST schedule as an initial solution for a local search / metaheuristic (e.g., swap-based neighborhood, critical-path guided adjustments).
18. Output the schedule: report start times, finish times, and makespan; optionally also report the resource usage profile and the order in which activities were scheduled.
Earliest Finish Time Priority Rule (EFT):
Input the RCPSP instance: number of activities (including dummy start/end), processing times, renewable resource types and capacities, non-renewable resource requirements (if any), and precedence relations (DAG).
Precompute for each activity its set of immediate predecessors and successors from the precedence constraints.
Initialize scheduling state: set current time t = 0; mark all activities as unscheduled; set remaining resource capacities to full capacities (for renewable resources) and record non-renewable consumption (if applicable).
Create data structures: (a) for each activity, store earliest feasible start time candidate (EFT candidate) and earliest finish time (EFT = candidate_start + duration); (b) maintain a set/list of eligible activities whose all predecessors are already scheduled; (c) maintain a priority queue (min-heap) keyed by earliest finish time (EFT).
Compute the initial eligible set: add all activities with no predecessors (typically the dummy start) to the eligible set.
For each activity in the eligible set, compute its earliest feasible start time under resource constraints: find the smallest time s >= t such that (i) all precedence constraints are satisfied (predecessors finished by s) and (ii) renewable resource availability is sufficient for the entire interval [s, s + duration).
For each eligible activity, compute its earliest finish time EFT = s + duration and insert/update it in the priority queue ordered by smallest EFT (tie-break consistently, e.g., by smaller activity id or larger duration).
Repeat until all real activities (and the dummy end, if used) are scheduled:
Pop from the priority queue the activity a with the smallest EFT (apply tie-break rules if multiple activities share the same EFT).
Re-validate feasibility at the popped time: if the stored earliest feasible start time is no longer feasible due to changes from previously scheduled activities (e.g., because the priority queue entry became stale), recompute the earliest feasible start time for activity a and update its EFT; if still feasible, proceed; otherwise reinsert with updated EFT.
Schedule activity a at its chosen earliest feasible start time s_a and finish time f_a = s_a + duration(a).
Update precedence status: mark activity a as scheduled; remove it from the eligible set; for each successor b of a, if all predecessors of b are now scheduled, add b to the eligible set.
Update renewable resource usage profile: for each renewable resource r, decrement capacity over the interval [s_a, f_a) (or record a resource usage timeline/interval list) so future feasibility checks reflect the new consumption.
If non-renewable resources exist: decrement remaining non-renewable capacities by the activity’s requirements at scheduling time; if any capacity becomes negative, the schedule is infeasible (in that case, the algorithm must backtrack or declare failure; EFT alone is typically used as a constructive heuristic assuming feasibility).
For each newly eligible activity b, compute its earliest feasible start time s_b given current scheduled activities and updated resource profiles; compute EFT_b = s_b + duration(b); insert b into the priority queue.
Also consider that some activities already in the priority queue might have their earliest feasible start times increased due to resource profile changes. To keep the algorithm correct, either (a) recompute feasibility when popped (lazy update), or (b) proactively update all affected queued activities (more expensive).
Advance the current time t if desired: set t = min start time among unscheduled eligible activities, or simply keep t as 0 and rely on feasibility checks; EFT selection already determines the next activity time.
Continue the loop until the dummy end activity is scheduled (or until all activities are scheduled).
Compute the objective value: for makespan, take the finish time of the dummy end (or max finish time over all activities). For other objectives (e.g., total resource usage, weighted completion), compute accordingly.
Return the schedule: list each activity’s start time and finish time, plus the objective value and feasibility status.
Optional enhancements for robustness: (a) use a deterministic tie-break rule (e.g., smaller id, larger duration, or smaller slack); (b) use efficient data structures for resource feasibility checks (e.g., segment trees or interval lists per resource); (c) add a feasibility repair step if the constructive schedule becomes infeasible due to non-renewable constraints.
Minimum Slack Priority Rule (MSP):
Define the RCPSP input data: set of activities V (including dummy start/end), renewable resource types R, nonrenewable resource types N (if any), processing times p_i, precedence constraints (i -> j), resource requirements req[i][k] for each renewable k in R, and reqN[i][m] for each nonrenewable m in N (if applicable), plus resource capacities cap[k] and capN[m].
Choose the scheduling horizon and representation: maintain for each activity i its earliest start time ES[i], latest start time LS[i] (if computing latest times), scheduled start time S[i], scheduled finish time F[i]=S[i]+p_i, and a status flag (unscheduled/scheduled).
Build the precedence graph: store predecessors Pred[i] and successors Succ[i] for every activity i from the given precedence constraints.
Compute a topological order of the precedence graph (for feasibility checks and for forward/backward passes).
Compute earliest feasible times (forward pass) to obtain ES[i]: set ES[start]=0 for the dummy start; for activities in topological order, set ES[j]=max(ES[j], ES[i]+p_i) for each precedence edge i->j; for dummy end, ES[end] is the earliest project completion under precedence only.
If using MSP exactly as a priority rule, compute latest feasible times (backward pass) to obtain LS[i]: set LS[end]=ES[end] (or a chosen project deadline if provided); traverse activities in reverse topological order; for each activity i, set LS[i]=min(LS[j]-p_i) over all successors j in Succ[i]; if an activity has no successors other than dummy end, use that accordingly.
Compute slack for each activity i: Slack[i]=LS[i]-ES[i]. (If LS is not computed, MSP can be approximated using a dynamic estimate of slack; however, the standard MSP uses LS and ES from the forward/backward passes.)
Initialize the schedule: set current time t=0; mark all activities unscheduled; set S[start]=0 and mark start as scheduled; update the set of eligible activities Eligible = {i | all predecessors of i are scheduled}.
Initialize resource availability profiles: for renewable resources, create a time-indexed or event-based structure to track remaining capacity over time; for nonrenewable resources, track remaining total amounts and deduct upon scheduling.
Repeat until all activities are scheduled:
  1) Determine the set of candidate activities C = Eligible that are feasible with respect to resources at their earliest possible start times. For each i in Eligible, compute its precedence-feasible earliest start candidate t0 = max(ES[i], max_{pred k of i}(F[k])) (with F[k] from the current partial schedule).
  2) Check renewable resource feasibility for activity i starting at t0: verify that for every time interval [t0, t0+p_i) and each renewable resource r, the required amount req[i][r] does not exceed remaining capacity. If using an event-based structure, check capacity constraints over the affected intervals.
  3) If nonrenewable resources exist, check that remaining nonrenewable capacity capN[m] - sum of already-consumed - reqN[i][m] remains nonnegative for all m when scheduling i.
  4) If i is feasible, include it in C; otherwise exclude it for now.
  5) If C is empty, advance time to the next event where resource availability or eligibility changes (e.g., the earliest finish time among currently running activities), update resource profiles accordingly, and recompute Eligible and C.
  6) If C is nonempty, select the activity i* with Minimum Slack Priority: i* = argmin_{i in C} Slack[i]. (Tie-breakers: choose the activity with smallest ES[i], then smallest index, or any deterministic rule.)
  7) Schedule i*: set S[i*]=t0 (the earliest feasible start time found for i*), set F[i*]=S[i*]+p_{i*}, and mark i* as scheduled.
  8) Update resource profiles: for renewable resources, decrement capacity by req[i*][r] over [S[i*], F[i*]); for nonrenewables, decrement remaining totals by reqN[i*][m].
  9) Update eligibility: remove i* from Eligible; for each successor j in Succ[i*], if all predecessors of j are now scheduled, add j to Eligible.
  10) (Optional but common) Update dynamic ES/Slack estimates if you want MSP to reflect the current partial schedule more tightly: recompute ES[j] based on the latest scheduled predecessor finish times; if you recompute ES, you may update Slack[j]=LS[j]-ES[j] for affected activities. (If you keep LS/ES from the initial forward/backward pass, you can keep Slack fixed.)
End repeat when all activities are scheduled (including dummy end).
Compute the objective value: project makespan = F[end]. If the RCPSP objective is different (e.g., minimize makespan under feasibility), compute accordingly.
Validate feasibility: ensure all precedence constraints are satisfied (by construction), renewable resource capacities are never exceeded, and nonrenewable constraints are respected (if present).
Return the resulting schedule (start times S[i] for all i) and the objective value (e.g., makespan).
Implementation notes for correctness and efficiency: use an event-based time representation to avoid large time-indexed arrays; maintain a priority queue keyed by (Slack, ES, id) for eligible feasible activities; when C is empty, jump to the next event time rather than incrementing t by 1; precompute LS and initial ES via forward/backward passes to obtain Slack quickly; if recomputing ES dynamically, update only affected successors to reduce overhead.
Most Critical Path Priority Rule (MCP):
1. Model the RCPSP instance: define activities (including dummy start/end), precedence constraints (DAG), renewable resource types and capacities, nonrenewable resources (if any), processing times, and resource demands per activity (renewable and nonrenewable).
2. Choose the scheduling horizon and representation: decide whether to use a time-indexed schedule or an event-based schedule; represent each activity with fields for earliest start time (EST), latest start time (LST) if needed, duration, resource demands, and predecessor/successor lists.
3. Compute precedence-based earliest start times (EST) ignoring resources: for each activity, set EST = max(finish times of all predecessors) using a topological order; initialize all activities as unscheduled.
4. Define the Most Critical Path Priority Rule (MCP) priority metric: for each unscheduled activity i, compute its remaining criticality measure, typically the length of the longest path from i to the project end (including processing times), i.e., MCP(i) = longest_path_duration(i → end) under precedence only.
5. Precompute longest-path values for MCP: perform a reverse topological traversal from the end dummy node to the start dummy node; for each activity i, set MCP(i) = duration(i) + max_{j in successors(i)} MCP(j), with MCP(end)=0. (If multiple end dummies exist, add a single dummy end.)
6. Initialize the schedule: set current time t = 0; create an empty schedule; set the start dummy as scheduled at time 0; mark it as completed; update the set of eligible activities.
7. Maintain the eligible set: at any decision point, define eligible activities as those whose all predecessors are already scheduled (completed).
8. At each decision iteration, select the next activity using MCP: from the eligible set, choose the activity k with the highest MCP(k) value (ties broken by a deterministic rule such as higher duration, higher resource demand, smaller index, or earliest EST).
9. Determine the earliest feasible start time for the selected activity: set candidate start s = EST(k) (computed from precedence and already scheduled predecessors).
10. Perform resource feasibility checking for renewable resources: for each time unit (or event interval) from s onward, verify that adding activity k would not exceed capacities for every renewable resource type at all times during [s, s + duration(k)).
11. If using event-based simulation, implement an efficient resource profile: maintain a resource usage timeline (e.g., arrays per resource over discrete time, or a set of change points) so that feasibility checks can be done by querying the maximum usage over the activity’s interval.
12. If nonrenewable resources exist, check feasibility once at start: ensure that total nonrenewable consumption up to the start time does not exceed capacities (or ensure cumulative consumption constraints are satisfied).
13. Find the earliest feasible start: if [s, s+duration(k)) is infeasible, increment s to the next time point where resource availability may improve (e.g., the next event time when some resource usage changes) and repeat feasibility checks until feasible.
14. Schedule the activity: once the earliest feasible start s* is found, assign start(k)=s* and finish(k)=s*+duration(k); add k to the schedule; mark k as completed.
15. Update precedence information: for each successor j of k, if all predecessors of j are now completed, add j to the eligible set; update EST(j) if you maintain it incrementally (EST(j)=max over completed predecessors’ finish times).
16. Update resource usage profile: apply the activity’s resource demands to the resource usage timeline over [s*, s*+duration(k)).
17. Advance the decision point: if there are still eligible activities, continue selecting the next activity immediately; otherwise, advance current time to the earliest time when at least one unscheduled activity becomes eligible (based on precedence) and/or when resources allow scheduling (depending on your implementation).
18. Repeat until completion: continue steps 7–17 until all activities (including the end dummy) are scheduled.
19. Ensure correctness for RCPSP constraints: verify that all precedence constraints are satisfied (start times respect predecessor finish times) and that all renewable/nonrenewable resource constraints are satisfied for the produced schedule.
20. Compute the objective value: for makespan minimization, compute makespan = finish(end dummy). For other objectives (e.g., total weighted completion time), compute accordingly.
21. Tie-breaking and determinism: implement consistent tie-breaking in step 8 to ensure reproducibility; optionally allow randomized tie-breaking to explore alternative schedules.
22. Optional improvement loop (common in practice): if the MCP schedule is used as a baseline, you may apply local search (e.g., shifting, swapping eligible activities, or re-running MCP with different tie-breakers) while preserving feasibility.
23. Complexity and performance considerations: precompute MCP values (step 5) once; use efficient data structures for resource feasibility checks (step 10–13); keep eligible set updates incremental to avoid repeated scans of all activities.
24. Output: return the activity start/finish times, the resource usage profile (optional), and the objective value (e.g., makespan).
Most Successors Priority Rule (MSU):
Input the RCPSP instance: activities V={1..n}, renewable resource types R, nonrenewable resources (if any), processing times p_i, resource requirements q_{ir} (renewable) and q_{i,nonrenew} (nonrenewable), precedence constraints (A→B), and the planning horizon/upper bound if provided.
Preprocess precedence constraints: compute for each activity i its set of immediate predecessors Pred(i) and immediate successors Succ(i).
Compute the successor count for each activity: for every activity i, compute SuccCount(i)=|{j in V : j is reachable from i via precedence paths}| (i.e., number of all transitive successors). If the rule is defined using only immediate successors, use |Succ(i)| instead.
Initialize scheduling state: set current time t=0; mark all activities as unscheduled; for each renewable resource r, initialize its capacity Cap_r and create a resource-availability profile (e.g., a time-indexed capacity array or an event-based structure).
Initialize the set of eligible activities Eligible = { i in V : Pred(i) is empty } (all predecessors already scheduled).
While there are unscheduled activities: (a) if Eligible is empty, advance time t to the next event time where some activity can become eligible (e.g., when a scheduled activity finishes and releases resources), update resource availability, and recompute Eligible; (b) otherwise proceed to select an activity.
Select the next activity to schedule using Most Successors Priority: choose i* in Eligible that maximizes SuccCount(i*). If ties occur, break them using a secondary rule (commonly earliest start time, largest processing time, smallest index, or highest total resource demand).
Compute the earliest feasible start time ES(i*) under precedence and renewable resource constraints: set s= max( completion time of all predecessors of i*, current time t ) and then find the smallest s' >= s such that for every renewable resource r and every time unit (or event interval) in [s', s'+p_{i*}), the remaining capacity after scheduling i* is nonnegative (i.e., does not violate Cap_r).
If the RCPSP includes nonrenewable resources, also check nonrenewable feasibility: ensure that the total nonrenewable consumption of all scheduled activities plus i* does not exceed the nonrenewable limits; if violated, do not schedule i* at this time (in practice, such instances are often handled by feasibility checks during selection or by using a different representation).
Schedule activity i*: set Start(i*)=s' and Finish(i*)=s'+p_{i*}. Mark i* as scheduled.
Update precedence eligibility: for each successor j in Succ(i*), decrement its remaining-predecessor counter; if all predecessors of j are now scheduled, add j to Eligible.
Update renewable resource availability: subtract q_{i*,r} from the resource profile over [Start(i*), Finish(i*)) for each renewable resource r (or update the event-based structure accordingly).
Update the current time t: set t to the minimum time among the next unscheduled decision point (often the earliest finish time among scheduled activities that could affect eligibility) or simply keep t as the current time and rely on the event-based recomputation when Eligible becomes empty.
Repeat the loop until all activities are scheduled (Eligible becomes empty and unscheduled set is empty).
Compute the makespan (project completion time) as Cmax = max_i Finish(i).
Return the schedule (Start/Finish times for all activities) and objective value (e.g., makespan).
Optional implementation details for efficiency: (a) maintain Eligible in a priority queue keyed by (-SuccCount(i), tie-breaker), (b) maintain a fast feasibility test for ES(i) using segment trees/bitsets for resource profiles, and (c) use event-based time advancement rather than unit time scanning.
Optional validation: verify all precedence constraints (Start(B) >= Finish(A) for each A→B) and all renewable resource constraints (capacity never exceeded) across the entire horizon.
Highest Level Priority Rule (HL):
1. Model the RCPSP: define activities (including dummy start/end), precedence constraints (A→B), renewable resource types and capacities, nonrenewable resources and limits (if applicable), processing times, and resource consumption per activity (renewable usage per time unit; nonrenewable total usage).
2. Choose the HL priority definition: for each activity i, compute its Highest Level (HL) value as the length of the longest path from i to the project end in the precedence graph, using processing times (and optionally including resource-dependent feasibility only if your variant requires it).
3. Build the precedence graph: store successors and predecessors for each activity; ensure the graph is acyclic (or preprocess to handle cycles).
4. Compute HL values (backward pass): starting from the end dummy activity, traverse activities in reverse topological order and set HL(end)=0; for any other activity i, set HL(i)=duration(i)+max_{j in succ(i)} HL(j) (if i has no successors, HL(i)=duration(i)).
5. Initialize the schedule: set current time t=0; mark all activities as unscheduled; set remaining resource capacities to full for renewable resources; set nonrenewable remaining to full limits (if used).
6. Create the initial eligible set E: include all activities whose predecessors are already scheduled (initially, those with no predecessors, typically the start dummy).
7. Define the selection rule (Highest Level Priority): when selecting the next activity to schedule, choose from E the activity with the maximum HL value; if ties occur, break ties using a deterministic secondary rule (e.g., earliest start time, largest duration, smallest index).
8. Define feasibility checks: for a candidate activity i and a proposed start time s, verify (a) precedence feasibility (all predecessors scheduled and finish time ≤ s), (b) renewable resource feasibility for every time unit in [s, s+duration(i)) that resource usage does not exceed capacities, and (c) nonrenewable feasibility that total nonrenewable usage accumulated after scheduling i does not exceed limits.
9. Determine the earliest feasible start time (ES) for the chosen activity: for the selected activity i, compute the earliest time s ≥ current time such that precedence constraints are satisfied and renewable/nonrenewable resource feasibility holds; if multiple time slots are possible, pick the smallest feasible s.
10. Schedule the activity: assign start(i)=s and finish(i)=s+duration(i); update the renewable resource usage profile over [s, finish(i)) and decrement nonrenewable remaining usage accordingly.
11. Update the set of scheduled/unscheduled activities: mark i as scheduled; remove i from E.
12. Update eligibility: for each successor k of i, if all predecessors of k are now scheduled, add k to E.
13. Advance time when needed: if E is empty (no eligible activities can be scheduled at the current time), advance t to the minimum finish time among currently running activities (or to the next event time) and update resource availability accordingly; then recompute/refresh E based on newly satisfied precedence conditions.
14. Handle multiple activities at the same time (optional but common): if your implementation allows parallel scheduling, after advancing to a time t, repeatedly select and schedule any activities from E that are feasible at time t (or at their earliest feasible start ≥ t), updating resources after each placement; continue until no further activity can be scheduled without violating feasibility.
15. Continue the main loop: repeat steps 7–14 until all activities (including the end dummy) are scheduled.
16. Compute the objective value: for makespan RCPSP, set makespan = finish(end dummy). For other objectives (e.g., total resource cost), compute accordingly.
17. Validate the schedule: verify precedence constraints, renewable resource capacities over time, and nonrenewable limits (if applicable). If infeasible, either (a) restart with different tie-breaking, (b) use a repair mechanism, or (c) adjust the earliest-start computation to ensure feasibility.
18. (Recommended) Add improvement options: optionally perform local search or schedule improvement (e.g., SGS variants, shifting, or re-running HL with different tie-breakers) to reduce makespan while keeping the HL priority framework.
19. Output results: return the activity start/finish times, the resource usage profile (if needed), and the final objective value (e.g., makespan).
Longest Processing Time Priority Rule (L:
1) Model the RCPSP instance: define activities/jobs V, precedence constraints (A→B), renewable resource types R, resource capacities Cap[r], and for each activity i: processing time p[i] and resource demands d[i][r].
2) Choose a scheduling horizon and representation: maintain for each activity i its start time S[i] (initially undefined), finish time F[i]=S[i]+p[i] (when scheduled), and a set of completed activities Done.
3) Compute the LPT priority key for each activity: set Priority[i] = p[i] (Longest Processing Time). If ties occur, break them deterministically (e.g., by earliest topological level, then by activity id).
4) Precompute precedence feasibility: for each activity i, store Pred[i] (immediate predecessors) and PredCount[i] (number of unsatisfied predecessors). Initialize PredCount[i] from the precedence graph.
5) Initialize the ready set Ready with all activities i such that PredCount[i]==0 (all predecessors already satisfied).
6) Initialize the current time t = 0 and an event structure for resource availability: for each resource r, maintain a timeline of occupied intervals (or a data structure that can test feasibility of adding an interval [s, s+p[i]) without exceeding Cap[r]).
7) While there are unscheduled activities (|Done| < |V|), repeat:
8) If Ready is empty, advance time to the next event where some activity completes (i.e., update resource timelines and update PredCount for successors whose predecessors finish), then recompute Ready.
9) Select the next activity to schedule using LPT: pick i in Ready with maximum Priority[i] (largest p[i]); if multiple, apply the tie-break rule.
10) Determine the earliest feasible start time for activity i under both precedence and renewable resource constraints:
11) Compute precedence earliest start: est_prec = max{ F[j] : j in Pred(i) } (or 0 if no predecessors).
12) Find the earliest time s >= est_prec such that for every renewable resource r, the interval [s, s+p[i]) can be inserted without violating Cap[r] given current resource usage (i.e., for all r, available capacity over that interval is sufficient).
13) Set S[i]=s and F[i]=s+p[i].
14) Commit the schedule decision: add activity i to Done; remove i from Ready.
15) Update resource timelines: for each resource r, record that activity i occupies d[i][r] units over [S[i], F[i]).
16) Update precedence status: for each successor k of i, decrement PredCount[k] by 1; if PredCount[k]==0, add k to Ready.
17) Update the current time t if your implementation uses a global time pointer; otherwise rely on the resource/precedence feasibility checks and event-driven updates.
18) Continue until all activities are scheduled.
19) Compute the makespan: Cmax = max{ F[i] : i in V }.
20) Output the schedule: return start times S[i], finish times F[i], and makespan Cmax (and optionally the activity order decisions).
21) Implementation notes for feasibility checking (resource constraints): implement a function Feasible(i, s) that tests all resources r by scanning the resource timeline for the maximum concurrent usage over [s, s+p[i]) and verifying it does not exceed Cap[r].
22) Implementation notes for earliest feasible start (time search): starting from est_prec, increment s to the next candidate time where resource availability changes (e.g., next completion time on any resource) to avoid step-by-step time scanning.
23) Complexity considerations: LPT itself is O(|V| log |V|) for priority selection if using a max-heap; the dominant cost is feasibility checking and earliest-start search, so optimize with efficient interval overlap queries or segment trees per resource.
